5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every morning, one of the first things I do is check on a collection of bright green cultures quietly growing inside glass photobioreactors. To most people, they might look like bottles filled with green water. To me, they represent years of scientific curiosity and one of the most exciting opportunities for a sustainable future.
Those tiny green cells are called microalgae.
Although invisible to the naked eye, they are remarkable organisms. They absorb carbon dioxide, produce oxygen through photosynthesis, and create valuable compounds that could one day help produce renewable fuels, nutritional supplements, biodegradable materials, and even medicines. It’s incredible to think that something so small could have such a big impact on our world.
But working with microalgae has taught me one important lesson.
Growing them isn’t easy.
A Tiny Organism with Big Demands
People often imagine that growing microalgae is as simple as placing them under a light and waiting for them to multiply.
I wish it were that straightforward.
Microalgae are surprisingly sensitive. Too much light can stress them. Too little carbon dioxide slows their growth. A slight change in temperature or nutrients can affect how quickly they produce biomass. Even changing one condition can influence several others at the same time.
As researchers, we constantly monitor these factors, making small adjustments in the hope of creating the perfect environment. Some days everything works beautifully. Other days, despite our best efforts, the culture simply refuses to behave as expected.
That’s the fascinating part of biology.
Living organisms don’t always follow our plans.
Why Artificial Intelligence Is Entering the Laboratory
This is where artificial intelligence becomes exciting — not because it replaces scientists, but because it helps us understand complexity.
Imagine trying to monitor hundreds of measurements every minute. Light intensity, temperature, pH, carbon dioxide, dissolved oxygen, nutrient levels, and cell growth are all changing continuously. Looking at this amount of information can quickly become overwhelming.
Artificial intelligence is exceptionally good at recognising patterns hidden inside large amounts of data.
Instead of spending hours searching for relationships between different measurements, AI can analyse thousands of data points in seconds. It can identify trends that humans might miss and even predict what is likely to happen next.
Think of it as another pair of eyes in the laboratory, eyes that never get tired.
Teaching AI to Think Ahead
One of the most exciting possibilities isn’t that AI can analyse today’s data.
It’s that it can help predict tomorrow’s.
Imagine noticing that a culture has already stopped growing. By that point, valuable time has been lost.
Now imagine an AI system recognising the earliest warning signs hours before growth begins to decline. It could recommend adjusting the light, increasing carbon dioxide, or modifying nutrient delivery before the problem becomes serious.
That’s the real power of AI.
Instead of reacting to problems, we begin preventing them.
For researchers, this means fewer failed experiments, more consistent results, and a better understanding of how microalgae respond to different environments.
More Than Just Better Research
Why does this matter outside the laboratory?
Because one of the biggest challenges facing microalgae isn’t scientific potential — it’s cost.
Scientists have known for years that microalgae can produce oils suitable for biofuels, valuable omega-3 fatty acids, proteins, pigments, and many other useful products. The difficulty has always been producing them efficiently enough to compete with existing technologies.
Every improvement in cultivation matters.
If AI helps cultures grow faster, reduces wasted energy, or detects problems before they become expensive failures, it brings commercial production one step closer to reality.
Small improvements inside a laboratory can eventually lead to big changes in industry.
A Cleaner Future
One reason I enjoy working with microalgae is that the research feels connected to something much bigger than the laboratory itself.
As microalgae grow, they consume carbon dioxide. They can be cultivated on land unsuitable for traditional farming, and some species can even grow using wastewater while recovering valuable nutrients.
Researchers are exploring their potential in sustainable aviation fuel, biodiesel, biodegradable plastics, food ingredients, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and wastewater treatment.
No single technology will solve climate change on its own.
But many small innovations working together can make a meaningful difference.
Artificial intelligence could become one of those innovations by helping us unlock more of what microalgae are already capable of doing.
The Scientist Is Still Essential
Whenever people hear about AI, the same question often comes up.
“Will it replace scientists?”
I don’t think so.
Artificial intelligence can analyse enormous amounts of information, but it doesn’t ask curious questions. It doesn’t design creative experiments. It doesn’t challenge old assumptions or imagine completely new ideas.
Those are still human strengths.
Instead of replacing researchers, AI becomes another tool — much like microscopes, sensors, or advanced laboratory equipment. It helps us work more efficiently, understand biological systems more deeply, and make better decisions.
The discoveries still begin with people asking, “What if?”
Looking Ahead
The future laboratory may look very different from today’s.
Photobioreactors could be connected to intelligent sensors that monitor every aspect of microalgal growth in real time. AI systems might continuously analyse incoming data, predict changes before they happen, and recommend the best operating conditions throughout the cultivation process.
Researchers would spend less time making routine adjustments and more time exploring new ideas.
That future may be closer than we think.
Around the world, scientists are already combining biology, engineering, data science, and artificial intelligence to build smarter cultivation systems. It’s an exciting time to be working in this field because every new discovery brings us one step closer to making sustainable technologies more practical and more affordable.
A Final Thought
When visitors walk into the laboratory, they often notice the bright green cultures first.
They see bottles filled with green liquid.
I see possibility.
I see microscopic organisms that have spent billions of years perfecting the art of turning sunlight and carbon dioxide into life. I also see a future where artificial intelligence helps us understand those organisms better than ever before.
Perhaps the fuel of the future won’t come from deep beneath the Earth’s surface.
Perhaps it will grow quietly inside a glass photobioreactor, guided by science, powered by sunlight, and supported by artificial intelligence.
Sometimes, the biggest ideas begin with the smallest living things.
